Home About Coursework Blog Contact

The Price Is Watching You

June 18, 2026
4

In 1985, just four things decided the price of a plane ticket.

hundreds

In 2026, it's hundreds.

And many of them are about you.

↓

It's the same plane ticket.

In 1985, four factors set its price: season, inventory, fuel cost, and what competitors charged. All about the market.

In 2026, it's hundreds. Most of them are about you.

1985 · Market
  • Season
  • Inventory
  • Fuel cost
  • Competitors
2026 · You

Hover or tap any factor.

Four factors describe the market. Hundreds describe you.
That difference is what this whole piece is about.

This isn't dynamic pricing.

Dynamic pricing has existed since the 1980s. Hotels cost more on weekends, flights cost more at Thanksgiving, supermarkets discount when stock piles up. Prices moved — but they moved for everyone at once.

What's different now is that the price no longer moves for the market. It moves for you. Two people can open the same app in the same second, search the same product, and see different prices — not because the market changed in that second, but because the algorithm decided they were different buyers.

The old question was: what is this product worth on the market?
The new question is: what is the most this person will pay?

Those are not the same question.
The gap between them is where your money goes.

So — what's your price?

The algorithm isn't deciding how much to charge you. It's deciding how much to charge someone like you. And "someone like you" is assembled from your device, location, time, and behavior. Each signal looks harmless on its own. Stacked together, they sketch a portrait — and that portrait sets the price you see.

Flip the five switches below and see what your portrait is worth.

Percentages are illustrative values calibrated from price gaps reported across multiple studies. The real numbers inside any given algorithm are trade secrets.

Baseline price fair market price
$12.00
Your algorithmic price
$15.00
Worst-case price a portrait flagged "easy to exploit"
$34.80

What just happened?

The algorithm read five signals about you and assembled a portrait. Each one barely moves the price on its own. A premium phone, maybe +8%. Low battery, another +12%. Late at night, +6%. A fifth search, +9%.

But stacked together, the same product can cost you twice what someone else pays.

You never see this math. You only see a price.

Sources

  1. Federal Trade Commission. "FTC Surveillance Pricing 6(b) Study: Research Summaries — A Staff Perspective." January 2025.
  2. Kumar, Krish, et al. "Research Problem Statement." Unpublished manuscript, 2026.
  3. McBride, John. Quoted in "The smart AI behind effective airline revenue management." PhocusWire, 17 December 2024.
  4. Mone, A., et al. "AI Price Tags and Privacy: When Your Data Sets Your Price." Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery, vol. 16, no. 1, 2026, p. e70070.
  5. Shiller, Benjamin Reed. "Approximating Purchase Propensities and Reservation Prices from Broad Consumer Tracking." International Economic Review, vol. 61, no. 2, 2020, pp. 847–870.
